sections.heading: What is MVVM?
sections.content: MVVM (Model-View-ViewModel) is a software architectural pattern that separates an application into three distinct layers: the Model (data and business logic), the View (UI layer — Activities, Fragments, XML), and the ViewModel (the bridge between Model and View that holds and manages UI-related data). MVVM is the officially recommended architecture for Android apps by Google, and it forms the foundation of the Android Jetpack component suite. Its core benefit is a clean separation of concerns — your UI code never directly touches your data sources, and your data logic never depends on Android UI classes.

sections.heading: Why MVVM for Android?
items: Separation of concerns — UI logic, business logic, and data access are clearly divided into separate layers
Testability — ViewModel and Model layers have no Android UI dependencies and can be unit tested on the JVM
Lifecycle awareness — ViewModel survives configuration changes (screen rotation) without re-fetching data
Reactive UI — View observes ViewModel data using LiveData or StateFlow and updates automatically
Single source of truth — ViewModel holds the UI state; the View only renders what the ViewModel exposes
Officially recommended by Google — all Jetpack components (ViewModel, LiveData, Room, Navigation) are designed for MVVM
Easier maintenance — changes to data sources or UI don't ripple across all layers
Supports unidirectional data flow — data flows down from ViewModel to View; events flow up from View to ViewModel

rows.responsibility: Represents the data and business logic of the app
rows.contains: Data classes, Repository, Room Database, Retrofit API service, DataStore
rows.android_classes: Data classes, Repository class, RoomDatabase, ApiService

rows.responsibility: Displays the UI and forwards user events to the ViewModel
rows.contains: XML layouts, Activities, Fragments — no business logic
rows.android_classes: Activity, Fragment, XML layout files, RecyclerView Adapters

rows.responsibility: Holds and manages UI state; exposes data to the View; handles user actions
rows.contains: LiveData/StateFlow, business logic calls, Repository interactions

sections.heading: MVVM Data Flow
sections.content: In MVVM, data flows in one direction: the Model provides data to the ViewModel, and the ViewModel exposes it to the View. The View observes the ViewModel's data streams (LiveData or StateFlow) and re-renders itself whenever the data changes. User interactions in the View trigger function calls on the ViewModel — never directly on the Model. The ViewModel then calls the Repository (part of the Model layer) to fetch, update, or delete data, and the result flows back up through the observable data stream to update the View automatically.

sections.heading: MVVM Data Flow Step by Step
items: 1. User interacts with the View (e.g., taps a button, types in a field)
2. View calls a function on the ViewModel (e.g., viewModel.loadUsers())
3. ViewModel calls the Repository to fetch or modify data
4. Repository fetches data from a local database (Room) or remote API (Retrofit)
5. Repository returns the result to the ViewModel (via suspend function, Flow, or callback)
6. ViewModel updates its LiveData or StateFlow with the new data or UI state
7. View observes the LiveData/StateFlow and automatically updates the UI to reflect the new state
8. The View never directly calls the Repository or Database — all data access goes through the ViewModel

rows.mvc: Yes — Activity/Fragment contains both UI and logic
rows.mvp: No — Presenter holds logic, View is passive
rows.mvvm: No — ViewModel holds logic, View only observes

rows.mvc: Hard — UI and logic are tightly coupled
rows.mvp: Good — Presenter can be tested with mock View
rows.mvvm: Excellent — ViewModel has no UI dependencies

rows.mvp: No — Presenter must be manually attached/detached
High — interface for every View action

sections.heading: The ViewModel Layer
sections.content: The ViewModel is the central piece of MVVM in Android. It is a lifecycle-aware class provided by the Jetpack ViewModel library that stores and manages UI-related data. The ViewModel is created the first time the system requests it and lives until the associated Activity or Fragment is permanently finished — not recreated. This means it automatically survives screen rotation, language changes, and other configuration changes, eliminating the need to refetch data or save complex state manually.

sections.heading: ViewModel Responsibilities
items: Hold UI state — expose data to the View using LiveData, StateFlow, or StateFlow<UiState>
Survive configuration changes — data is retained across screen rotations without re-fetching
Handle user events — expose functions that the View calls in response to user interactions
Call the Repository — request data operations (fetch, insert, update, delete) from the data layer
Apply business logic — filter, sort, transform, or combine data before exposing it to the View
Manage loading and error states — expose a UiState sealed class to represent loading, success, and error
Never reference Activity, Fragment, or View — doing so causes memory leaks and breaks testability
Never perform UI operations — no Toast, no Navigation calls — delegate these back to the View via events

sections.heading: The Model Layer — What It Contains

rows.description: Plain Kotlin data classes representing entities (e.g., User, Product, Order)
rows.example: data class User(val id: Int, val name: String, val email: String)

rows.description: Single source of truth for data — abstracts local and remote data sources from the ViewModel
rows.example: class UserRepository(private val dao: UserDao, private val api: UserApi)

rows.description: Data Access Object — defines database operations as Kotlin functions
rows.example: interface UserDao { @Query("SELECT * FROM users") fun getAllUsers(): Flow<List<User>> }

rows.description: Interface defining HTTP endpoints for remote data fetching
rows.example: interface UserApiService { @GET("users") suspend fun getUsers(): List<User> }

rows.description: Local key-value storage for user preferences and settings

sections.heading: The View Layer Rules
items: The View (Activity or Fragment) is responsible only for rendering UI and capturing user input
Observe LiveData or StateFlow from the ViewModel and update Views accordingly
Forward all user actions to the ViewModel — do not handle business logic in the View
Use viewLifecycleOwner (not 'this') when observing in Fragments to prevent stale observers
Never instantiate Repository or data sources directly in the View — always go through ViewModel
Keep UI logic minimal — if you're writing more than a few lines of logic in a Fragment, it likely belongs in the ViewModel
Use sealed class UiState to handle loading, success, and error states driven by the ViewModel
Navigation and dialogs can be triggered from the View in response to ViewModel events (SingleLiveEvent or Channel)

rows.description: Data is being fetched — show a ProgressBar or shimmer
rows.sealed_class: sealed class UiState { object Loading : UiState() }

rows.description: Data fetched successfully — show the content
rows.sealed_class: data class Success<T>(val data: T) : UiState()

rows.description: Something went wrong — show an error message and retry option
rows.sealed_class: data class Error(val message: String) : UiState()

rows.description: Request succeeded but no data exists — show an empty state illustration

sections.heading: LiveData vs StateFlow in ViewModel
items: LiveData is lifecycle-aware — only delivers updates when the observer is in STARTED or RESUMED state
StateFlow always has a value — it requires an initial value and exposes the current state at any time
StateFlow is a Kotlin coroutines construct — it integrates naturally with suspend functions and Flow
LiveData is simpler to set up for beginners — no coroutine scope needed in the View
StateFlow requires collecting with repeatOnLifecycle(Lifecycle.State.STARTED) in Fragments for lifecycle safety
Use MutableLiveData internally in ViewModel, expose as LiveData to the View
Use MutableStateFlow internally, expose as StateFlow to the View
SharedFlow is used for one-time events (navigation, Snackbar) that should not replay on recomposition

rows.livedata: Built-in — only delivers to active observers
rows.stateflow: Manual — use repeatOnLifecycle() in the View
Required — must provide an initial value

rows.livedata: Limited — use liveData {} builder or asLiveData()
Simple state in beginner or legacy MVVM projects
rows.stateflow: Modern projects — especially with Jetpack Compose

sections.heading: Repository Pattern in MVVM
items: The Repository is the single source of truth — it decides whether to return cached local data or fetch fresh remote data
The ViewModel only talks to the Repository — it has no knowledge of Room, Retrofit, or DataStore directly
Repository abstracts the data source — swapping Room for Realm or Retrofit for Ktor requires no ViewModel changes
Repository coordinates multiple data sources: check local cache first, fetch from network if stale or missing
Expose data as Flow<T> from the Repository so the ViewModel and View react to changes automatically
Repository is typically injected into the ViewModel using Hilt dependency injection
Each feature area typically has its own Repository (UserRepository, ProductRepository, OrderRepository)
Repository is a plain Kotlin class — no Android dependencies — making it easily unit testable

rows.contains: Activities, Fragments, XML layouts, RecyclerView Adapters, ViewModels
Repository classes, Room database, DAO interfaces, API service interfaces
ApiService.kt, Retrofit instance, DTOs (Data Transfer Objects)
Use case classes that encapsulate single business operations
rows.layer: Domain — between Model and ViewModel (used in Clean Architecture)

rows.contains: Hilt modules for dependency injection — provides Repository, Database, ApiService
Data classes representing business entities (User, Product, Order)

sections.heading: One-Time Events from ViewModel to View
items: Some actions should happen once — show a Snackbar, navigate to another screen, show a dialog
LiveData is not ideal for one-time events — it replays the last value to new observers (e.g., after rotation)
SingleLiveEvent is a common workaround — a LiveData subclass that only delivers to one observer once
SharedFlow with replay=0 is the modern coroutine-based approach for one-time events
Channel<Event> with receiveAsFlow() is another clean pattern for one-time UI events
In the View, collect one-time events in a separate flow collection alongside the UI state
Event types: NavigateToDetail, ShowSnackbar, ShowDialog, RequestPermission — model as a sealed class
Never trigger navigation or Snackbar directly from the ViewModel — expose events and let the View react

rows.mistake: Holding Activity or Fragment reference in ViewModel
rows.fix: Never store View references in ViewModel — use AndroidViewModel only for applicationContext if needed

rows.mistake: Calling Repository directly from Activity or Fragment
rows.fix: All data access must go through the ViewModel — View only observes data

rows.mistake: Putting business logic in the Fragment
rows.fix: Move filtering, sorting, and transformation logic to the ViewModel or Repository

rows.mistake: Using LiveData for one-time events without SingleLiveEvent
rows.fix: Use SharedFlow(replay=0) or Channel for events that should fire exactly once

rows.mistake: Observing LiveData with 'this' (Fragment) instead of viewLifecycleOwner
rows.fix: Always use viewLifecycleOwner in Fragments to prevent stale observers after back stack return
Use by viewModels() or by activityViewModels() delegate — let Jetpack manage the lifecycle

sections.heading: Summary
sections.content: MVVM is not just a pattern — it is the foundation of scalable, testable, and maintainable Android development. By keeping your View dumb (it only observes and renders), your ViewModel focused (it manages state and delegates data operations), and your Model clean (it provides data through Repositories), you build apps that are easy to test, easy to change, and resilient to Android lifecycle complexity. Every major Jetpack component — ViewModel, LiveData, Room, Navigation, Hilt — is designed to work within the MVVM pattern, making it the natural choice for modern Android.
